title: FPV Drone Cinematic Reel - Alex Vanover
creator: Alex Vanover (Captain Vanover)
award_or_showcase_provenance: Not a juried-contest or festival selection for this specific video. Independently corroborated via WebSearch this session (multiple independent sources: NBC 5 Dallas-Fort Worth profile piece, Dallas Observer profile, GamesBeat, Forbes 'Alex Vanover, 23, FPV Drone Pilot' feature, dronedj.com/dronexl.co/Collider/SlashFilm coverage of the 2022 film 'Ambulance'): the creator, Alex Vanover, was the 2019 Drone Racing League (DRL) World Champion (the first rookie to win the title) and subsequently worked as a professional FPV cinematographer on Michael Bay's feature films 'Ambulance' (2022) and 'Emancipation', plus music videos for Justin Bieber/Kid Laroi ('Stay') and Karol G. This is a genuine, well-corroborated creator-reputation/reach signal (elite competitive pedigree + Hollywood production credits), not a specific award or festival placement for this particular showreel upload.
notes: CANDIDATE-SOURCED, INDEPENDENTLY RE-VERIFIED THIS SESSION -- one new exemplar diversifying this pack's FPV-showreel/edit-forward subgenre (distinct from the pack's existing single-shot FPV clips, e.g. flaviufpv-madeira-fanal-forest-fpv.json, drone-racing-league-waterfall-fpv-dive.json, which document one continuous take rather than a multi-clip montage). Verified this session via YouTube's oEmbed endpoint (https://www.youtube.com/oembed?url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0wjsF-WUbrM&format=json), which returned title='FPV Drone Cinematic Reel - Alex Vanover', author_name='Captain Vanover', author_url='https://www.youtube.com/@captainvanover' -- an exact field-for-field match to the candidate's title and source_url; the candidate's fuller creator attribution 'Alex Vanover (Captain Vanover)' was independently corroborated via WebSearch this session against multiple unrelated outlets (NBC 5 Dallas-Fort Worth, Dallas Observer, GamesBeat, Forbes, dronedj.com), which consistently identify the individual behind the 'Captain Vanover' channel/handle as Alex Vanover, a Fort Worth, Texas-based professional FPV pilot -- so the oEmbed channel name and the candidate's individual-name attribution are corroborated as referring to the same person, not asserted on the candidate's word alone. A direct curl fetch of the raw watch-page HTML (desktop User-Agent, ~1.37MB of markup) this session confirmed: lengthSeconds='120'/approxDurationMs='120120' (duration set to 120.12s below, the first verified/confirmed duration in this pack rather than left null, since the raw player-response JSON was successfully extracted this session, unlike prior attempts on other exemplars where this extraction failed), publishDate='2021-06-29T10:25:09-07:00' (dateText 'Jun 29, 2021'), viewCount='81780', category='Film & Animation', and the full shortDescription text. A case-insensitive grep of the same raw HTML for 'creative commons', 'licensedForReuse', 'cc-by', 'attribution license', 'licenseName', and 'standard youtube license' found zero matches anywhere in the page (same negative-evidence method used for every other exemplar in this pack) -- per this project's stated default (assume all-rights-reserved unless the uploader's license setting is directly confirmed otherwise), license_category is set to 'all-rights-reserved'. This is further reinforced by the video's own description text, which states 'I do not claim ownership to this music' -- i.e. the upload itself contains third-party-owned background music, an independent signal against any blanket open-license claim on this upload. Consequently local_media_path stays null and no video file was downloaded or persisted anywhere in this repo, consistent with the spec's metadata-first default and its explicit prohibition on bulk-downloading all-rights-reserved footage; flagged_for_user_signoff is not applicable here since the license-verified-but-withheld case (constraint 3's cc-by/cc0/public-domain/owned branch) never triggered -- this record defaulted to all-rights-reserved, the standard (non-signoff) path. The extracted shortDescription independently confirms the 'edit-forward reel' premise supplied in the candidate brief: it credits 'Patrick and Brendan over at Lightcraft.TV for putting this reel together' (i.e. this is a professionally assembled, multi-clip montage edited by a third party, not a single raw take) and states 'the majority of this video was shot on the Red Komodo, with some shots on the GH-5 and the rest on a GoPro' -- i.e. multiple cameras/formats were intercut into one reel, corroborating a genuine multi-shot, edit-forward structure rather than a guess. award_or_showcase_provenance documents the creator's independently-corroborated 2019 DRL World Championship title and subsequent professional film credits (Michael Bay's 'Ambulance'/'Emancipation', music videos) -- a real, multi-source-corroborated creator-reputation signal, though explicitly not a juried award for this specific upload (none was found or claimed). scores (sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness) are scores_provenance='manually_estimated' judgments, NOT measurements from Capability 1's actual scoring functions (scoring_sharpness.py/scoring_exposure.py/scoring_motion_smoothness.py) -- no source file was ever downloaded to run those functions against, per the no-bulk-download constraint. Calibration reasoning: sharpness=0.80 sits at this pack's upper tier (comparable to dronestagram-feature.json's 0.85 and skypixel11_africa_unseen's 0.8), justified by the disclosed camera stack -- the Red Komodo is a professional 6K RAW cinema camera (a materially stronger, disclosed hardware signal than the undisclosed-camera FPV exemplars elsewhere in this pack), intercut with a GH-5 and GoPro. exposure=0.76 is set slightly more conservatively than sharpness because exposure/dynamic-range quality on FPV freestyle footage (frequently shot in mixed outdoor lighting, fast-moving, sometimes backlit) is not independently confirmed by any camera-spec disclosure, so it sits mid-upper rather than top-tier. motion_smoothness=0.83 is the highest of the three, above the 0.80 given to the less-corroborated drone-racing-league-waterfall-fpv-dive.json exemplar, on a stronger and more specific basis than that record's generic 'FPV cinematic convention' reasoning: this creator's elite, independently-verified competitive pedigree (2019 DRL World Champion, the top tier of competitive FPV piloting, which directly demands and demonstrates fine motor/throttle control) plus a named professional edit house (Lightcraft.TV) assembling the reel together are both genuine, disclosed, above-baseline signals for controlled, deliberate flight and polished post-production stabilization -- still a qualitative judgment, not a frame-level measurement. composite_score IS a real computation, not hand-estimated: produced this session by calling this repo's own drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.composite.compute_composite_score with these three manually-estimated inputs (sharpness=0.80, exposure=0.76, motion_smoothness=0.83, composition=None) and drone_video_ai.highlight_extraction.weights.default_weights(weights_version='default-v1') (equal 1/3 weighting on sharpness/exposure/motion_smoothness, composition weight fixed at 0.0) via '.venv/bin/python' in this session, returning 0.7966666666666666 (rounded to 0.7967 here); because composition is None, the function's None-skipping guard excludes it from both the weighted sum and the renormalization denominator, so the result reduces to the equal-weighted average of the three present signals: (0.80+0.76+0.83)/3 = 0.7966666666666666.

editorial_style.pacing_notes: format='short-form-reel' is grounded in text/title/description evidence gathered this session, not a guess: the video's own title is 'FPV Drone Cinematic Reel' (self-described as a reel, not a single continuous shot or documentary-style long-form piece), and its extracted shortDescription explicitly credits a named third party ('Patrick and Brendan over at Lightcraft.TV') with 'putting this reel together' from footage shot on three different cameras (Red Komodo, GH-5, GoPro) -- i.e. a montage assembled from multiple discrete clips, matching this pack's 'short-form-reel' definition (edit-forward, multi-clip, music-cut) as distinct from a single unbroken cinematic take. Note this classification is based on editorial/structural style, not runtime: at ~120.12s (verified via this session's raw-HTML lengthSeconds/approxDurationMs extraction) this upload is longer than a platform-native Short/Reel (<60s) and is a standard 16:9 YouTube watch-page upload, not a /shorts/ URL -- so 'short-form-reel' here describes the multi-clip montage editing convention the candidate brief calls out ('rapid, rhythmically-cut aerial clips edited to music rather than raw single-take footage'), not the platform's Shorts product. No live playback or scrubbing of the video was attempted in this session (browser automation tools were available but not invoked, consistent with this pack's existing precedent for reel-style exemplars of not exercising an interactive browser session for a single-record bounded verification task), so estimated_cut_count, avg_shot_length_seconds, and transition_styles_observed stay null/empty rather than guessed -- no oEmbed, WebFetch, WebSearch, or raw-HTML result in this session described the exact cut count, individual shot durations, or specific transition techniques (whip pans, hard cuts, speed ramps, etc.) used in this specific edit. review_method is set to 'text_provenance_only' to reflect that format/pacing conclusions come from title/description/metadata text, not actual viewing.
